$comment: Generative news-article corpus for the 1960s (telegram / trackside wire-report voice), parsed by Companion.Core.News.NewsArticleBank. Keys under 'bodies' are 'journalPhase|cause'; each holds era-keyed body templates selected deterministically via the 'headlines' PCG32 stream (a pure function of masterSeed + round). Bodies compose literal prose, fact tokens, and {pool:name} references into 'pools', so a handful of templates and pools yield thousands of distinct dispatches. Fact tokens: {player} {team} {race} {year} {round} {position} {expected} {winner} {fieldSize} {champPosition} {champMove} {champLeader}. Add new eras/events by adding JSON only, no code. 1960s copy is uppercase-leaning wire-report prose, STOP-punctuated where a telegram would break.

1960s: FROM THE CIRCUIT, WIRE REPORT.
TRACKSIDE DISPATCH, {race}, {year}.
BY TELEGRAPH FROM THE PADDOCK.
OUR CORRESPONDENT CABLES FROM {race}.
WIRE FILED AT THE FALL OF THE FLAG.
CABLED FROM THE PIT LANE, {race}.
OUR CORRESPONDENT AT {race} WIRES THIS DISPATCH.
FILED BY TELEGRAPH, ROUND {round} OF {year}.
FROM THE GRANDSTAND RAIL, A WIRE REPORT.
DISPATCH FROM {race}, THE INK STILL WET. STOP.
BY CABLE FROM THE {year} PADDOCK.
WIRED AS THE DUST SETTLED AT {race}.
The garage celebrates long into the evening. STOP.
Rivals will note the name; the season is far from run. STOP.
A drive the crowd will not soon forget.
The record book keeps the rest.
More of the same, and the paddock will talk of little else.
The works will sleep easy this night. STOP.
A name for the headlines, and deservedly so.
The paddock lifts its cap to a fine drive.
Such afternoons are why drivers take to the wheel.
The team packs up with heads held high. STOP.
One for the scrapbook, and the mantelpiece besides.
The crowd went home well satisfied.
The mechanics wheel the car away in silence. STOP.
There will be quieter words back at the works.
A weekend the team will wish to forget.
Better days lie ahead, the paddock trusts.
The stopwatch tells no kind story this day.
The transporter rolls for home under a grey sky. STOP.
Fortune turned her face away this weekend.
The engineers have a long night's reckoning ahead.
Racing gives, and racing takes; today it took.
A page best turned quickly, and soon forgotten.
The pit board reads only regret this afternoon. STOP.
The team trusts the luck will even out in time.
default: The team regroups for the next round.

1960s: In the championship {player} sits {champPosition}, {champMove}.

As for the title, {player} holds {champPosition}, {champMove}.
The points table places {player} {champPosition}, {champMove} since last we wired.
For the championship, read {player} at {champPosition}, {champMove}.
against a car reckoned good only for {expected}

from a machine the timekeepers pegged at {expected}
when the form book promised only {expected}

past all expectation of {expected}

{winner} proved strongest at the sharp end
{winner} supplied the afternoon's winning drive
{winner} held sway where it counted most
{winner} carried the day at the head of the field
it was {winner} who took the honours
The timing sheets passed from hand to hand along the pit counter.
Stopwatches clicked and pencils moved as the paddock made its reckoning.
The pit boards came down and the result was entered in every notebook.
Mechanics and timekeepers alike paused to study the final order.
The result had reached the telegraph desk before the transporters were loaded.
Around the paddock, the final classification supplied the evening's talk.
The board at race control settled the matter for every watching team.
By dusk, the figures were copied into notebooks from one end of the paddock to the other.
default: The timing sheets told the story.
The final order supplied plenty of conversation.
The teams packed up with the result recorded.

1960s: Back in the pits, the mechanics began their checks without delay.
The machine was wheeled back for a long evening's inspection.
Spanners came out as soon as the car returned to the {team} pit.
The works would receive a careful report before the transporter reached home.
Oil-stained notebooks filled as the crew searched for an answer.
At the pit counter, the crew spoke quietly and turned to diagnosis.
The machine returned to the transporter with a long list for the factory.
The mechanics set to work while the rest of the paddock packed for home.
default: The team began its checks immediately.
The crew turned to the work ahead.
The garage began searching for answers.

1960s: The paddock scatters to the winter, the record book closed for another year. STOP.
Until the flag falls again, next season.
A campaign for the history books, whatever comes next.
The garages fall dark till spring. STOP.
The wire falls silent until the new campaign.
Another year written into the annals of the sport.
The mechanics turn to the winter's toil, and to next year.
So closes the book on a season none will forget. STOP.
default: The year is in the books.
